issues.title: Unvalidated query parameters injected into PostgREST filters
issues.description: The seller reports route accepts `buyerId`, `status`, `currency`, `amountMin`, `amountMax`, `dateFrom`, `dateTo` from `searchParams` and injects them directly into PostgREST query filters without format validation. While `sellerId` is safely scoped to `user.id` (line 117), `buyerId` is not validated as a UUID, and `status`/`currency` accept arbitrary strings. URLSearchParams encoding prevents PostgREST operator injection, but non-UUID `buyerId` values are silently accepted and produce no-match results rather than an error — a potential information disclosure surface if PostgREST behavior changes or if an attacker probes filter behavior. The admin route (`apps/admin/src/app/api/admin/reports/orders/route.ts`) validates dates and amounts but similarly does not validate UUID format for `sellerId`, `buyerId`, or `productId`.
issues.fix: Add UUID format validation for `buyerId`, `sellerId`, and `productId` parameters using the existing `validateUuid` / `UUID_REGEX` utility before constructing the PostgREST filter. Add an allowlist check for `status` and `currency` fields against known valid enum values.

issues.title: Stock validation compares requested quantity to static max_quantity (not remaining stock)
issues.description: The order creation route validates `item.quantity > product.max_quantity`, where `max_quantity` is a static capacity field on the product. It does NOT account for already-sold/reserved units. The actual remaining stock check (`reserve_stock` RPC) is done in `checkoutQueries.ts` via a Supabase RPC which properly decrements the stock atomically. However, the API route in `apps/payments/src/app/api/checkout/orders/route.ts` (which appears to be an older or alternative implementation path) uses only the static `max_quantity` field. This could allow overselling if the API route is used directly instead of the RPC-based path in `checkoutQueries.ts`. The checkout flow must go through the RPC to be safe.
issues.fix: Remove or deprecate the static `max_quantity` check in the API route, or supplement it with a check against `supabase.rpc('reserve_stock')` as done in `checkoutQueries.ts`. Add a comment making clear that this route does not atomically reserve stock and should not be used for production checkout.

issues.title: File type validation for receipt uploads is client-side only
issues.description: Receipt file validation (`assertValidReceiptFile`, `validateReceiptFile`) is performed exclusively on the client side before uploading directly to Supabase Storage. There is no server-side API route that validates the MIME type or file size before storing. A malicious user could bypass the client-side check and upload any file type (e.g., HTML, SVG with scripts, executables) directly to the `receipts` bucket using the Supabase client SDK with a valid session token. The stored file URL is then displayed in `ReceivedOrderCard` as an `` or `` — an SVG with embedded JavaScript or a phishing HTML page could be harmful if served from Supabase's CDN.
issues.fix: Add a server-side API route or Supabase Storage policy that validates file MIME type and size. Alternatively, configure Supabase Storage bucket policies to only allow `image/jpeg`, `image/png`, and `image/webp` MIME types. Also consider serving receipts through a signed URL proxy that strips content-type or forces download disposition.

issues.title: Content Security Policy uses 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' in script-src across all apps
issues.description: All 7 apps (studio, landing, payments, playground, auth, admin, store) configure their CSP with `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval'`. This effectively disables the XSS protection benefit of CSP for scripts. `'unsafe-eval'` allows eval()-based code execution, and `'unsafe-inline'` allows inline event handlers and script tags. Combined, these directives mean any XSS vulnerability would not be mitigated by CSP. The same pattern is repeated in all apps: `apps/auth/next.config.ts`, `apps/admin/next.config.ts`, `apps/store/next.config.ts`, `apps/payments/next.config.ts`, `apps/landing/next.config.ts`, `apps/playground/next.config.ts`.
issues.fix: Replace `'unsafe-inline'` and `'unsafe-eval'` with a nonce-based or hash-based CSP. Next.js supports nonce-based CSP via middleware. `'unsafe-eval'` may be required by some third-party libraries but should be removed where possible. At minimum, document why these directives are necessary.

issues.title: Hardcoded default seller email and production URL in seed script
issues.description: The seed script has `const SELLER_EMAIL = process.env.SELLER_EMAIL || 'furrycolombia@gmail.com'` and a hardcoded `IMG_BASE = 'https://moonfest.furrycolombia.com'` plus a hardcoded console.log pointing to `https://store.furrycolombia.com/store`. This is a portability and data sensitivity concern. The default email means the script could accidentally seed production data for a specific user if `SELLER_EMAIL` is not set. The hardcoded production URL is committed to source control.
issues.fix: Remove the default fallback for `SELLER_EMAIL` and require the env var explicitly (fail fast if not set). Move `IMG_BASE` and production URLs to env vars or accept them as CLI arguments. This follows the same pattern already used for `SUPABASE_URL` and `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY`.

issues.title: dangerouslySetInnerHTML used with a static, controlled string — safe
issues.description: The `ThemeScript` component uses `dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: themeScript }}` where `themeScript` is a hardcoded JavaScript string constant defined in the same file. The content is entirely developer-controlled, does not include any user input, and does not interpolate external values. This is the standard Next.js pattern for injecting a blocking theme initialization script. No action required.
issues.fix: No fix required. This is a safe and intentional use of dangerouslySetInnerHTML with a static, developer-controlled string.

issues.title: CORS implementation is origin-allowlist-based — correct pattern
issues.description: The auth refresh route implements CORS by comparing the `Origin` header against a set of allowed origins built from environment variables (`NEXT_PUBLIC_AUTH_URL`, `NEXT_PUBLIC_STORE_URL`, etc.). Only matching origins get `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` set to the request origin. This is the correct secure pattern. No wildcard origins are used. Credentials are allowed only for known origins.
issues.fix: No fix required. The CORS implementation follows security best practices.

issues.title: Checkout payment methods route correctly gates on permissions and stock
issues.description: The route correctly: (1) verifies session via `createServerSupabaseClient().auth.getUser()`, (2) validates all input UUIDs via `UUID_REGEX`, (3) checks that the user has both `orders.create` and `receipts.create` permissions, (4) validates stock server-side and returns `{ methods: [], hasStockIssues: true }` if any item exceeds stock — not exposing payment details. This correctly implements the checkout stock integrity requirement.
issues.fix: No fix required. The stock integrity pattern is correctly implemented.

issues.title: Order creation uses atomic stock reservation via RPC — correct pattern
issues.description: The `createOrder` function uses `supabase.rpc('reserve_stock')` for atomic stock reservation with proper rollback (release_stock on failure), and calculates order total server-side from DB prices to prevent price manipulation via cart cookie manipulation. This is the correct, secure pattern.
issues.fix: No fix required. The atomic stock reservation and server-side price calculation are correctly implemented.
